The Five Funding Cliffs Approaching as Shutdown Pain Builds — WSJ
data.description:
By Anvee Bhutani

WASHINGTON — Federal workers, including air-traffic controllers, are set to miss full paychecks soon and food-aid programs are bracing to run out of money, as a series of funding cliffs tied to the government shutdown start to pinch more workers and families.

The shortfalls in coming days and weeks — plus closely watched elections in a handful of states — could raise the pressure on lawmakers and President Trump to resolve the three-week-old impasse. Democrats have vowed they won't vote for any short-term spending bill to reopen the government unless Republicans first agree to extend healthcare subsidies.

The shutdown is "having real impacts on American families," said House Majority Leader Steve Scalise (R., La.) at a news conference on Wednesday. He said Democrats are holding out because they "want to show their far-left radical base that they hate President Trump."

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D., N.Y.) countered that Republicans "would rather keep the government closed than provide healthcare that's affordable to working-class Americans."

Some pain from the shutdown has been delayed, after the Trump administration shifted funds to keep military servicemembers and some other workers from missing a paycheck. It also covered a shortfall in WIC, the Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants and Children. It is possible the administration could do so again, but such funds are expected to become scarcer.

Here is the string of looming deadlines, which threaten to raise the stakes and could force lawmakers and Trump to come to the negotiating table.

Oct. 24

Missed paychecks: Many federal employees are expected to miss their first full paychecks since the shutdown began, heightening public pressure. Federal workers already received only partial paychecks earlier in October, and many are continuing to work without pay. (Payment dates vary slightly by agency.) Already, lines at food banks are growing in areas with large populations of government workers.

Trump's Asia trip: On the same day, Trump departs for a trip to Asia, potentially shifting the political spotlight overseas. Both Democratic and Republican leaders have signaled that negotiations can only make effective progress if the White House is on board.

Oct. 28

More missed paychecks: Air-traffic controllers, who are working without pay, are set to miss an entire paycheck on this date alongside other federal workers. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy has warned that absenteeism is rising as the shutdown drags on. Concerns about airport safety and delays helped bring an end to the shutdown during Trump's first term.

There is a push in Washington to make sure air-traffic controllers get paid, either through unilateral action by the White House or through legislation from Senate Commerce Committee Chairman Ted Cruz (R., Texas). So far, GOP leaders haven't scheduled a vote. If such a bill did pass the Senate, the House would have to return to Washington to take it up.

Oct. 31

Military pay: Active-duty military personnel are due their next paycheck on Oct. 31, and it isn't clear whether the Trump administration will find new funds to cover the next payroll. The White House made funds available so service members didn't miss their paycheck on Oct. 15. Some lawmakers are also trying to pass interim appropriations to pay the military, but such proposals are unlikely to advance.

Nov. 1

ACA subsidies: The open-enrollment period for Affordable Care Act health plans begins. Millions of Americans shopping for 2026 coverage are set to see their costs rise as pandemic-era enhanced subsidies expire and overall premiums increase. Democrats have sought to extend ACA subsidies, which are set to expire after December, at a cost of tens of billions of dollars a year. But Republicans say they won't negotiate on healthcare until Democrats vote to reopen the government.

SNAP benefits: The Agriculture Department has warned that the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program won't be able to provide full benefits in November. SNAP, which relies on federal funding but is administered by states, provides benefits that can be used like cash to help low-income families purchase food. More than 41 million Americans are enrolled in SNAP. States have already started warning residents they are unlikely to get any benefits in November if the shutdown persists.

Head Start: The federal low-income preschool program is currently operating on reserve funding that is set to run out, putting more than 65,000 children at risk of losing services.

Nov. 4

State elections: Voters in Virginia and New Jersey wrap up voting in their off-year gubernatorial contests. The election could serve as a test of how voters feel about Washington dysfunction, and the result could bolster or undercut each party's negotiating leverage during the shutdown.

If Democrats perform strongly, they will argue that voters are punishing the GOP for the shutdown and that Republicans must compromise. If Republicans hold their ground or improve, the party might feel emboldened to press harder. Either way, the election results will show how much room each side has to maneuver.

This explanatory article might be updated periodically.

Write to Anvee Bhutani at anvee.bhutani@wsj.com

data.title: Personal loan provider World Acceptance's Q2 revenue beats estimates on loan growth
data.description:
Overview

  • World Acceptance Corp fiscal Q2 2026 revenue beats analyst expectations, growing 2.3% yr/yr

  • Company reports net loss of $0.38 per share due to bond redemption expenses

  • Company repurchased 7.4% of outstanding shares, totaling $66.6 mln

Outlook

  • Company expects improved results in fiscal 2027 and beyond due to strategic investments

  • Company anticipates continued improvement in portfolio gross and net yield

Result Drivers

  • LOAN GROWTH - Co reports 1.5% year-over-year growth in outstanding loans, first growth since September 2022

  • INCREASED CUSTOMER BASE - Customer base grew by 6.2% over the past year, supporting loan growth

  • INTEREST AND FEE INCOME - Interest, fee and insurance income increased by $4.6 mln, or 3.6%, with a 136 basis point yield increase

Key Details

Metric

Q2 Revenue

Beat/Miss

Beat

Actual

$134.50 mln

Consensus Estimate

$127.85 mln (2 Analysts)

Analyst Coverage

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is no "strong buy" or "buy", 2 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell"

  • The average consensus recommendation for the consumer lending peer group is "buy."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for World Acceptance Corp is $157.50, about 14% below its October 21 closing price of $179.60

  • The stock recently traded at 14 times the next 12-month earnings vs. a P/E of 12 three months ago

Press Release:

data.title: Swiss 10-Year Bond Yield Hovers at 2022-Lows
data.description:
The yield on the 10-year Swiss government bond was around 0.14%, hovering near its lowest since January 2022, amid reduced expectations of policy easing in the foreseeable future by the Swiss National Bank.

In the minutes of its last monetary policy meeting held on September 25, the central bank downplayed deflationary pressures and ruled out moving into negative rates for now.

data.title: Cinema operator IMAX beats Q3 revenue, profit expectations
data.description:
Overview

  • IMAX Q3 revenue rises 16.6% yr/yr to $106.7 mln, beating analysts' expectations

  • Adjusted EPS and net income for Q3 also beat consensus

  • Company reports record Q3 cash flow of $67.5 mln from operating activities

Outlook

  • IMAX remains on track for $1.2 bln in global box office in 2025

  • Company expects system installations to reach high end of 2025 guidance of 150-160

  • IMAX anticipates at least four major releases in 2026

Result Drivers

  • GLOBAL BOX OFFICE - IMAX achieved its highest grossing Q3 ever with $368 mln, up 50% YoY, driven by diverse content including local language films

  • SYSTEM INSTALLATIONS - Demand for IMAX systems remains strong with installations surpassing 2024 totals and pacing to high-end of full year guidance

  • CONTENT DIVERSITY - Diverse content mix, including local language and alternative content, drove significant box office growth

Key Details

Metric

Beat/Miss

Actual

Consensus Estimate

Q3 Revenue

Beat

$106.70 mln

$105.15 mln (12 Analysts)

Q3 Adjusted EPS

Beat

$0.47

$0.37

$26.20 mln

$18.44 mln (7 Analysts)

Q3 Net Income

$22.60 mln

Q3 Gross Margin

63.10%

Q3 Adjusted EBITDA Margin

48.60%

Analyst Coverage

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 10 "strong buy" or "buy", 1 "hold" and 1 "sell" or "strong sell"

  • The average consensus recommendation for the entertainment production peer group is "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Imax Corp is $38.50, about 16.8% above its October 21 closing price of $32.05

  • The stock recently traded at 21 times the next 12-month earnings vs. a P/E of 22 three months ago
